old_claim.note: Photography provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Public Newspaper Advertising provides the paid-periodical notice practice that mass advertising scaled through larger newspapers, magazines, posters, brands, and retail campaigns.

source_shape.source_locator: The Massachusetts Historical Society notes that the Boston News-Letter solicited paid notices in 1704 and carried the first paid American newspaper advertisements two weeks later.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Paid newspaper notices provide the periodical advertising practice that later mass advertising scaled.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source documents newspaper advertising as an established paid periodical practice before industrial-era mass advertising, while photography is not required for the 1837 newspaper, magazine, poster, and retail campaign scope.

ontology_before: Early mass advertising depended directly on photography, overstating a visual reproduction technology that was not required for the 1837 mass-periodical advertising scope.
ontology_after: Early mass advertising depends on public_newspaper_advertising as the narrower paid-notice practice that scaled through mass newspapers, magazines, posters, brands, and retail campaigns.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: advertising_mass_media_early now depends on public_newspaper_advertising and no longer directly depends on photography.
would_reject_if: The node is rescoped to photographic display advertising rather than early industrial mass advertising.
A stronger source shows photography was a direct required method for the 1837 mass-advertising scope.
A more precise paid_periodical_advertising node is added and replaces public_newspaper_advertising.
short_reason: The direct lineage is paid newspaper advertising, not photography.
